title: The Estuary's Extended Monologue

features.name: tidal-pool-archive
features.desc-on-touch: Salt water holds its own versions of what fresh water forgot. You listen to layers: the voice of the turning tide, the argument between two water-kinds, the archive's preference for both.

features.name: mangrove-signal-post
features.desc-on-touch: A wooden post, salt-scarred, broadcasting tidal tables and estuary warnings to whoever reads past the static. The archive has learned to speak in the rhythm of the tide.

features.name: salt-crust-scatter
features.desc-on-touch: Mineral deposits where water dried, leaving behind the names of things the estuary has already forgotten. Each crystal is a file the archive accidentally let the salt remake.

features.name: echo-chamber-arch
features.desc-on-touch: Where the river mouth opens into the harbour, sound folds back on itself. You hear the tide listing what it has taken. The archive mirrors it back, corrupting the inventory with each retelling.

features.name: brackish-ground-sprite
features.desc-on-touch: A creature that lives in the moment between salt and fresh, never committing to either. It moves the way compromise moves—sideways, always about to leave.

features.name: sediment-stack
features.desc-on-touch: Layers of what the estuary has sorted from upstream—mulch, ash, lost letters, the material evidence that all rivers argue with the sea before they mix. The archive keeps each layer separate and indexed.

inscription: salt water keeps its grudges longer than the archive can forget them

file.name: Estuary_Tidal_Record_NSW_1970s-Present.mp3
file.note: Hourly tide broadcasts and estuary brine-shrimp chorus, archived locally since the 70s. Corruption note: layers have begun playing simultaneously; you hear six different tide-times at once.

exits.label: the way back to where water still argues
deeper into the estuary where the river forgets its name
following salt-trace lines back into the paddocks
seaward, where the estuary ends but the salt memory continues
